claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 18 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and in approved education
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 17 and in approved education; Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is 17 and in approved education
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period
circumstances: Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin the Child Benefit eligibility assessment.

**First, I need to know about your child's age.**

How old is the child you're claiming Child Benefit for?

- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

Please let me know which age category applies to your child. (If you have multiple children, we can go through each one - just let me know!)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Hello! Yes, I'm interested in claiming Child Benefit and would like to know if I'm eligible.

I actually have two children I'd like to ask about. Alex is 17, and Blake is 18. Should I tell you about both of them, or would you like to go through them one at a time?

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! Thank you for that context. Yes, please feel free to tell me about both Alex (17) and Blake (18) as we go through the assessment. Since they're both in the 16-19 age range, they'll have similar eligibility requirements.

For children aged 16-19, eligibility for Child Benefit depends on whether they're in approved education or training. Let me navigate to the relevant checks.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
For **Alex (17)** and **Blake (18)**, I need to know about their current education or training status.

For each child, please tell me which applies:

1. **Currently in approved education or training** - such as A-levels, T-levels, Scottish Highers, NVQs up to level 3, or similar qualifications (not university/higher education)

2. **Recently left education or training** - within the last few months

3. **Neither** - not in education/training and hasn't been recently

Could you let me know the situation for both Alex and Blake?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Sure! 

Alex is studying for his A-levels at sixth form college, so he's still in education.

Blake finished school earlier this year - he's not studying anything at the moment and isn't in any training programs either.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that clear information! Let me assess each child's situation.

**For Alex (17)** - studying A-levels at sixth form college, this sounds like approved education. Let me verify the education level requirements.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Alex** - A-levels are on the approved qualifications list. I just need to confirm a couple of things:

1. Is Alex studying **full-time** (at least 12 hours per week of supervised study)?
2. Is Alex **working full-time** (defined as paid work of 24+ hours per week) alongside his studies?

---

**For Blake (18)** - since he's not currently in education or training, I need to check if he might qualify for an extension period. Let me look at that pathway.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Let me get back to the correct node to check Blake's extension eligibility:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for your patience. Here's where we are:

---

**For Alex (17)** - A-levels at sixth form college is approved education. Just to confirm:
1. Is Alex studying **full-time** (at least 12 hours per week)?
2. Is Alex also **working 24 or more hours per week** in paid employment?

---

**For Blake (18)** - The news here is less positive, I'm afraid. There is a **20-week extension** available for children who've left education, but this extension:
- Only applies to children aged **16-17** (Blake is 18)
- Ends when the child turns **18**, whichever comes sooner

Since Blake is already 18 and is not in approved education or training, he unfortunately **would not qualify** for Child Benefit.

---

Could you please confirm the details about Alex's study hours and work status so I can complete his assessment?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Yes, Alex is studying full-time - he's at college most days during the week. And no, he doesn't work at all, so certainly not 24 hours a week.

That's disappointing about Blake. So just to be clear, even though he only turned 18 recently and finished school this year, because he's already 18 now, there's no way he qualifies?

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for confirming Alex's details. Let me continue Alex's assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good progress on Alex! A couple more quick questions:

**For Alex:**
1. Does Alex receive any benefits in his own name, such as **Employment and Support Allowance (ESA)** or **Universal Credit**?

---

**Regarding Blake** - I completely understand your disappointment, and it's a fair question. Let me be absolutely clear about the rules:

The **20-week extension period** is specifically designed for children who leave education and are looking for work or training. However, the rules state that this extension:
- Is only available while the child is aged **16 or 17**
- **Ends when the child turns 18**, whichever comes first

So unfortunately, yes - because Blake has already turned 18 and is not currently in approved education or training, there is no pathway to eligibility under the current rules. If Blake had registered with a careers service (like Connexions or a government careers service) *before* turning 18, the extension would have ended on his 18th birthday anyway.

I'm sorry I don't have better news about Blake. Would you like me to continue with Alex's assessment?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
No, Alex doesn't receive any benefits in his own name - nothing like that.

And yes, I understand about Blake now. That's a shame, but thank you for explaining it clearly. Let's finish up with Alex's assessment then.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Alex doesn't receive benefits in his own name. One more question about Alex:

**Is Alex on an apprenticeship?** 

Since you mentioned he's studying A-levels at sixth form college, I assume the answer is no - but I just want to confirm he's not doing any kind of apprenticeship alongside his studies?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not on an apprenticeship - just doing his A-levels at college, that's all.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Now I need to ask about your residency status.

**Do you meet UK residency requirements?**

You qualify if **any** of the following apply to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK**
2. You have **settled status** under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have **pre-settled status** and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold)
4. You have **pre-settled status** and are actively seeking work (up to 91 days)
5. You have **pre-settled status** with sufficient resources to support yourself
6. You have **pre-settled status** and are a student with sufficient resources
7. You have **pre-settled status** as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national
8. You're a **Crown servant** living abroad

Do any of these apply to you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, living in Manchester means you meet the UK residency requirement.

**Are you responsible for Alex?**

You qualify if **any** of the following apply:

1. **You live with Alex**
2. **You contribute to Alex's upkeep** (e.g., paying for food, clothes, pocket money, housing) - at least the equivalent value of Child Benefit
3. **You pay maintenance** for Alex under a court order or agreement

Which of these applies to your situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

For example, this could be:
- Another parent or guardian
- Someone Alex has lived with previously

If there is another claimant, you'll both need to agree on who should receive the benefit. If there's a dispute, HMRC will make the final decision.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Nearly there! Just a couple more questions.

**What is Alex's current living situation?**

- **Living normally at home** with you
- **In hospital** for an extended stay
- **In local authority care**
- **In a fostering arrangement**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex is living normally at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
One final question:

**Is the local council paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

(For example, if Alex were receiving financial support from the council for housing or living costs)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the council isn't paying anything towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Wonderful news! I've completed the assessment. Let me provide you with the final outcome for both children.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
---

## 🎉 Assessment Complete

### **Alex (age 17) — ELIGIBLE ✅**

Great news! You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex. He qualifies because:
- He's aged 16-19 and in **approved full-time education** (A-levels)
- He's not working 24+ hours per week
- He doesn't receive benefits in his own name
- You live in the UK and Alex lives with you
- No one else is claiming for him
- The council isn't paying towards his accommodation

### **Blake (age 18) — NOT ELIGIBLE ❌**

Unfortunately, you cannot claim Child Benefit for Blake because:
- He's 18 and not in approved education or training
- The 20-week extension for school leavers only applies to 16-17 year olds

---

## Next Steps for Alex

1. **Apply online** at [gov.uk/child-benefit](https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim) or by post using form CH2
2. **Documents you'll need:**
   - Proof of your identity
   - Alex's birth certificate
3. **Payments** are made every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receive certain benefits)

### ⚠️ Important Note
If you or your partner earns over **£60,000**, you may be subject to the **High Income Child Benefit Charge**. You can still claim, but some or all of the benefit may need to be repaid through your tax return. It's worth checking if this applies to you.

---

Is there anything else you'd like me to clarify about the assessment or the application process?

reasonings: Alex (17) is ELIGIBLE for Child Benefit because: (1) He is aged 16-19 and in approved full-time education (A-levels at sixth form college, which is non-advanced education with 12+ hours per week); (2) He is not working 24+ hours per week; (3) He does not receive benefits in his own name (ESA/Universal Credit); (4) He is not on a disqualifying apprenticeship; (5) The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester); (6) The claimant is responsible for Alex (lives with him); (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex; (8) Alex is living normally at home; (9) The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance.

Blake (18) is NOT ELIGIBLE for Child Benefit because: He is aged 18 and is not currently in approved education or training. The 20-week extension period for children who have left education only applies while the child is aged 16 or 17, and ends when they turn 18. Since Blake has already turned 18 and is not in approved education or training, there is no qualifying pathway for eligibility.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has two children: Alex (17) and Blake (18). Alex qualifies for Child Benefit as he meets all requirements - he's in approved full-time education (A-levels), not working full-time, and the claimant meets all residency and responsibility criteria. Blake does not qualify because at 18 and not in approved education/training, he falls outside the eligibility rules. The 20-week extension for school leavers only applies to 16-17 year olds and ends at age 18.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.description: For 16-19 year olds, check if in education/training or eligible for extension
response.routing_logic: If in education/training, validate type and level. If just left education (within 20 weeks), check extension eligibility.

response.question: Is the child in approved (non-advanced) education or training?
response.help_text: Approved education: A-levels, T-levels, NVQ Level 3 or below, Scottish Highers/Advanced Highers. Advanced (NOT approved): University degrees, HNC, HND, NVQ Level 4+. Must be full-time (12+ hours/week supervised study).

criteria.must_not_be_married_unless_dependent: Married 16-19 year olds only qualify if financially dependent on claimant

age_limits.birthday_rule: Benefit stops on child's 20th birthday, not end of academic year

training_exclusions: Training with employer agreement for future job

payment_rates.note: Current rates not specified in source documents - check gov.uk for latest rates

high_income_charge.note: High Income Child Benefit Charge applies if you or partner earns over £60,000. This is a payment adjustment, not an eligibility criterion.

response.question: Has the child (aged 16-17) left education within the last 20 weeks AND registered with a qualifying service?
response.help_text: 20-week extension available if child has left approved education/training and registered with: government careers service, Connexions, or armed services. Extension ends at week 20 or when child turns 18, whichever is sooner.

response.question: Is the child in approved (non-advanced) education or training?
response.help_text: Approved education: A-levels, T-levels, NVQ Level 3 or below, Scottish Highers/Advanced Highers. Advanced (NOT approved): University degrees, HNC, HND, NVQ Level 4+. Must be full-time (12+ hours/week supervised study).

criteria.must_not_be_married_unless_dependent: Married 16-19 year olds only qualify if financially dependent on claimant

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements

Provide proof of identity for yourself and the child
Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex (17) is ELIGIBLE for Child Benefit because: (1) He is aged 16-19 and in approved full-time education (A-levels at sixth form college, which is non-advanced education with 12+ hours per week); (2) He is not working 24+ hours per week; (3) He does not receive benefits in his own name (ESA/Universal Credit); (4) He is not on a disqualifying apprenticeship; (5) The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester); (6) The claimant is responsible for Alex (lives with him); (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex; (8) Alex is living normally at home; (9) The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance.

Blake (18) is NOT ELIGIBLE for Child Benefit because: He is aged 18 and is not currently in approved education or training. The 20-week extension period for children who have left education only applies while the child is aged 16 or 17, and ends when they turn 18. Since Blake has already turned 18 and is not in approved education or training, there is no qualifying pathway for eligibility.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant has two children: Alex (17) and Blake (18). Alex qualifies for Child Benefit as he meets all requirements - he's in approved full-time education (A-levels), not working full-time, and the claimant meets all residency and responsibility criteria. Blake does not qualify because at 18 and not in approved education/training, he falls outside the eligibility rules. The 20-week extension for school leavers only applies to 16-17 year olds and ends at age 18.
